agent_output: {"executive_summary":"The deployed architecture has three critical integration points: Order→Payment (sync HTTP), Payment→Stripe (sync HTTP to external gateway), and Order→Notification (async fire-and-forget). The synchronous chain from Order through Payment to Stripe creates a deep coupling where any failure cascades upstream, and the fire-and-forget notification pattern silently drops emails. This analysis enumerates 9 distinct failure modes and prescribes a layered resilience strategy using circuit breakers, retries with backoff, idempotency keys, transactional outbox, and timeout budgets.","architecture":{"components":[{"name":"Order Service","responsibility":"Accepts customer orders, orchestrates payment charging, and triggers notification. Owns order lifecycle and state transitions.","boundaries":"Owns Order aggregate. Must not embed payment or notification logic — only coordinates via interfaces."},{"name":"Payment Service","responsibility":"Abstracts payment processing. Translates internal payment requests into Stripe API calls. Owns payment state (pending, charged, failed, refunded).","boundaries":"Owns Payment aggregate. Encapsulates all Stripe-specific logic behind an anti-corruption layer. Never exposes Stripe concepts upstream."},{"name":"Notification Service","responsibility":"Sends confirmation emails and other customer communications.","boundaries":"Owns notification templates and delivery tracking. Stateless from caller's perspective but should internally track delivery state."},{"name":"Stripe (External Gateway)","responsibility":"Third-party payment processing. Outside our control — availability, latency, and behavior are external constraints.","boundaries":"External system. All interactions must be treated as unreliable by default."}],"interfaces":[{"from":"Order Service","to":"Payment Service","contract":"POST /payments {orderId, amount, currency, idempotencyKey} → {paymentId, status, failureReason?}","coupling_type":"synchronous-temporal — Order blocks until Payment responds"},{"from":"Payment Service","to":"Stripe","contract":"Stripe Charges API with idempotency key passthrough","coupling_type":"synchronous-temporal — Payment blocks until Stripe responds"},{"from":"Order Service","to":"Notification Service","contract":"POST /notifications {orderId, type, recipient} — fire-and-forget, no response awaited","coupling_type":"async-fire-and-forget — no delivery guarantee, no backpressure"}],"data_flow":"Customer → Order Service → (sync) Payment Service → (sync) Stripe Gateway. On payment success, Order Service → (fire-and-forget) Notification Service → Email Provider. The critical path is the synchronous chain; notification is off the critical path but currently has no delivery guarantee.","patterns_used":[{"pattern":"Circuit Breaker","rationale":"Prevents cascade failures when Payment Service or Stripe is down. Order Service stops calling Payment after repeated failures, failing fast instead of exhausting resources."},{"pattern":"Retry with Exponential Backoff + Jitter","rationale":"Handles transient failures (network blips, 503s) on both the Order→Payment and Payment→Stripe hops without causing thundering herd."},{"pattern":"Idempotency Keys","rationale":"Critical for payment safety. Retries must not double-charge. Stripe natively supports idempotency keys; Payment Service must propagate them and also be idempotent itself."},{"pattern":"Timeout Budget (Deadline Propagation)","rationale":"Prevents unbounded waits in the synchronous chain. Order Service sets an overall deadline; Payment Service gets remaining budget minus overhead, ensuring the entire chain respects the caller's patience."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget for notifications. Order Service writes a notification event to an outbox table in the same transaction as the order state change, then a relay process delivers it to Notification Service with at-least-once guarantee."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead (Connection Pool Isolation)","rationale":"Isolates the HTTP connection pool for Payment calls from other outbound calls in Order Service, preventing a slow Payment Service from starving unrelated operations."},{"pattern":"Fallback / Graceful Degradation","rationale":"When circuit is open for payments, Order Service can accept orders in a 'payment-pending' state and process them when the circuit closes, rather than rejecting all orders."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Ordering dictates what needs to be paid; Payments serves that need but owns how it is processed."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Stripe (External)","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Payment Service translates between internal payment model and Stripe's API, shielding the domain from external model changes."},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Transactional Outbox — Ordering publishes order events; Notifications consumes them with at-least-once delivery."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than moving to async saga","gains":"Simpler mental model. Immediate payment confirmation to user. No saga orchestrator or compensating transactions needed.","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service availability. Latency is additive across the chain.","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with choreography — decouples availability but adds eventual consistency complexity and compensating transactions","Async saga with orchestrator — clean control flow but introduces a stateful orchestrator component"]},{"decision":"Transactional Outbox for notifications instead of fire-and-forget","gains":"At-least-once delivery guarantee. No silent email loss. Notification delivery is atomic with order state change.","sacrifices":"Adds an outbox table and a polling/CDC relay process. Slightly more operational complexity.","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/SQS) — adds infrastructure dependency but stronger delivery semantics","Keep fire-and-forget with scheduled reconciliation job — simpler but reactive rather than proactive"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker with graceful degradation (accept orders in pending state) vs hard rejection","gains":"Higher order acceptance rate during payment outages. Better customer experience. Orders can be retried when payment recovers.","sacrifices":"Introduces a 'payment-pending' state that needs background processing. Risk of accepting orders that ultimately can't be paid.","alternatives_considered":["Hard reject all orders when circuit is open — simpler but loses revenue during outages","Queue orders for later processing — similar to pending state but with message broker dependency"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"The ACL in Payment Service means Stripe can be swapped for another gateway (Adyen, Braintree) without changes to Order Service. Add a PaymentGateway interface and swap implementations."},{"area":"Notification channel","evolution_path":"Transactional outbox decouples notification trigger from delivery mechanism. Can add SMS, push notifications, or webhooks by adding consumers to the outbox relay without touching Order Service."},{"area":"Payment flow (sync → async)","evolution_path":"If payment latency or availability becomes untenable, the synchronous call can be migrated to an async saga. The idempotency keys and Payment aggregate state machine already support this — only the coordination pattern changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe chain","reason":"By design — we chose synchronous for simplicity and immediate confirmation. This means Order Service uptime is bounded by Payment Service and Stripe uptime. The circuit breaker and graceful degradation mitigate but don't eliminate this coupling."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once established, the idempotency key format and propagation path must remain stable. Changing it risks double-charges or lost payments. This contract is intentionally rigid."},{"area":"Outbox table schema","reason":"The outbox table is a shared contract between the Order Service write path and the relay process. Schema changes require coordinated deployment."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Double-charging: Retry on timeout where Stripe actually charged but response was lost","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Mandatory idempotency keys on every payment request. Payment Service stores Stripe's idempotency key before calling Stripe. On retry, Stripe deduplicates. Payment Service also deduplicates on its own idempotency key. Order Service must generate a deterministic idempotency key per order (e.g., order-{orderId}-payment-{attempt})."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss: Fire-and-forget HTTP POST drops emails with no trace","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox pattern. Order state change and outbox write are in the same DB transaction. Relay process polls or uses CDC to deliver to Notification Service with retries. Dead-letter after N failures for manual review."},{"risk":"Cascade failure: Stripe degradation causes Payment Service thread/connection exhaustion, which cascades to Order Service thread/connection exhaustion","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Three-layer defense: (1) Timeout budgets — Payment Service times out Stripe calls at 5s, Order Service times out Payment calls at 8s. (2) Circuit breaker — after 5 failures in 30s, circuit opens for 60s. (3) Bulkhead — dedicated connection pool for payment calls, capped at 50% of total capacity."},{"risk":"Partial failure inconsistency: Payment succeeds at Stripe but Order Service fails to update order status (crash between payment response and DB write)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Payment Service is the source of truth for payment state. Order Service should query Payment Service on startup/recovery to reconcile. A periodic reconciliation job compares Order states with Payment states and fixes inconsistencies."},{"risk":"Thundering herd on circuit close: When circuit breaker transitions from open to half-open, a burst of retried requests can overwhelm a recovering Payment Service","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Half-open state allows only a single probe request. Use exponential backoff with jitter on the circuit breaker's reset timer. Consider a token-bucket rate limiter in half-open state."},{"risk":"Outbox relay lag: High order volume causes outbox table to grow faster than the relay can process, delaying notifications","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Monitor outbox lag as a key metric. Scale relay workers horizontally. Partition outbox by order ID for parallel processing. Alert when lag exceeds SLA (e.g., 5 minutes)."},{"risk":"Stripe API contract change: Breaking change in Stripe's API causes Payment Service failures","severity":"medium","mitigation":"ACL in Payment Service isolates Stripe-specific logic. Pin Stripe API version. Monitor Stripe changelog. Integration tests against Stripe's test mode in CI."},{"risk":"Timeout misconfiguration: Order Service timeout shorter than Payment Service timeout, causing Order Service to give up while Payment Service is still processing","severity":"high","mitigation":"Enforce timeout budget hierarchy: Order Service timeout > Payment Service timeout > Stripe call timeout. Example: Order=10s, Payment=8s, Stripe=5s. Validate this in configuration, not just convention."},{"risk":"Idempotency key reuse across different operations: Developer error reuses an idempotency key, causing Stripe to return a cached response for a different charge","severity":"high","mitigation":"Generate idempotency keys deterministically from (orderId + paymentAttempt + amount). Validate uniqueness in Payment Service. Reject requests where idempotency key exists but parameters differ (Stripe does this natively, but validate internally too)."}],"implementation_guidance":{"recommended_approach":"Implement resilience in three phases. Phase 1 (immediate, highest impact): Add idempotency keys end-to-end and replace fire-and-forget notifications with a transactional outbox. These fix the two most dangerous silent failure modes. Phase 2 (next sprint): Add circuit breaker on Order→Payment call and timeout budget hierarchy across the synchronous chain. Phase 3 (hardening): Add bulkhead isolation, reconciliation jobs, and observability (circuit state dashboards, outbox lag metrics, payment reconciliation reports).","files_to_create_or_modify":["order-service/src/clients/PaymentClient.{ts,java} — add circuit breaker, timeout, retry with backoff, idempotency key generation","order-service/src/domain/Order.{ts,java} — add 'payment-pending' state for graceful degradation","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxTable.sql — create outbox table schema (id, aggregate_id, event_type, payload, created_at, published_at)","order-service/src/infrastructure/OutboxRelay.{ts,java} — poll outbox table and deliver to Notification Service with retries","payment-service/src/clients/StripeAdapter.{ts,java} — ACL wrapping Stripe SDK with timeout and idempotency key passthrough","payment-service/src/domain/Payment.{ts,java} — add idempotency key storage and deduplication logic","payment-service/src/api/PaymentsController.{ts,java} — accept and validate idempotency key header","shared/config/resilience.yaml — centralized timeout budget and circuit breaker configuration","order-service/src/jobs/PaymentReconciliation.{ts,java} — periodic job to reconcile order vs payment states"],"patterns_to_follow":"Use a resilience library (Resilience4j for Java, cockatiel/opossum for Node.js, Polly for .NET) rather than hand-rolling circuit breakers. Wrap Stripe calls behind a gateway interface for testability. Make the outbox relay idempotent — Notification Service must handle duplicate deliveries. Use structured logging with correlation IDs across all three services to trace failures through the synchronous chain.","pitfalls_to_avoid":["Do NOT retry payment calls without idempotency keys — this is how double-charging happens","Do NOT set the same timeout at every level of the chain — enforce a decreasing timeout budget from outer to inner service","Do NOT use in-memory circuit breaker state in a multi-instance deployment without considering that each instance tracks independently — this is acceptable but be aware thresholds are per-instance","Do NOT add a message broker just for notifications if a transactional outbox suffices — avoid premature infrastructure complexity","Do NOT treat Stripe webhook delivery as a substitute for your own idempotency — webhooks can be delayed or lost too","Do NOT log full payment details (card numbers, CVVs) in error traces — mask sensitive fields"]},"escalations":[],"confidence":{"level":88,"high_confidence_areas":["Failure mode enumeration — these are well-understood distributed systems failure patterns","Idempotency key design — Stripe's native support makes this straightforward","Transactional outbox replacing fire-and-forget — well-established pattern with clear implementation path","Timeout budget hierarchy — standard practice for synchronous chains"],"low_confidence_areas":["Graceful degradation (payment-pending state) — whether the business accepts the risk of orders that may never be paid depends on domain rules not provided","Specific timeout values — optimal values depend on observed p99 latencies which require production data","Circuit breaker thresholds — failure count and window size need tuning based on actual traffic patterns"],"assumptions":["Order Service and Payment Service each have their own database (no shared DB)","Services run as multiple instances behind a load balancer","Stripe is the only payment gateway (no multi-gateway routing needed currently)","Email is the only notification channel currently","The business prefers accepting orders during payment outages over rejecting them (graceful degradation)"]}}

Stripe natively supports idempotency keys; Payment Service must propagate them and also be idempotent itself."},{"pattern":"Timeout Budget (Deadline Propagation)","rationale":"Prevents unbounded waits in the synchronous chain. Order Service sets an overall deadline; Payment Service gets remaining budget minus overhead, ensuring the entire chain respects the caller's patience."},{"pattern":"Transactional Outbox","rationale":"Replaces fire-and-forget for notifications. Order Service writes a notification event to an outbox table in the same transaction as the order state change, then a relay process delivers it to Notification Service with at-least-once guarantee."},{"pattern":"Bulkhead (Connection Pool Isolation)","rationale":"Isolates the HTTP connection pool for Payment calls from other outbound calls in Order Service, preventing a slow Payment Service from starving unrelated operations."},{"pattern":"Fallback / Graceful Degradation","rationale":"When circuit is open for payments, Order Service can accept orders in a 'payment-pending' state and process them when the circuit closes, rather than rejecting all orders."}]},"domain_model":{"bounded_contexts":[{"name":"Ordering","classification":"core","aggregates":["Order"]},{"name":"Payments","classification":"core","aggregates":["Payment"]},{"name":"Notifications","classification":"supporting","aggregates":["NotificationRequest"]}],"context_map":[{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Payments","relationship":"Customer-Supplier — Ordering dictates what needs to be paid; Payments serves that need but owns how it is processed."},{"upstream":"Payments","downstream":"Stripe (External)","relationship":"Anti-Corruption Layer — Payment Service translates between internal payment model and Stripe's API, shielding the domain from external model changes."},{"upstream":"Ordering","downstream":"Notifications","relationship":"Customer-Supplier via Transactional Outbox — Ordering publishes order events; Notifications consumes them with at-least-once delivery."}]},"trade_offs":[{"decision":"Keep Order→Payment synchronous rather than moving to async saga","gains":"Simpler mental model. Immediate payment confirmation to user. No saga orchestrator or compensating transactions needed.","sacrifices":"Order Service availability is coupled to Payment Service availability. Latency is additive across the chain.","alternatives_considered":["Async saga with choreography — decouples availability but adds eventual consistency complexity and compensating transactions","Async saga with orchestrator — clean control flow but introduces a stateful orchestrator component"]},{"decision":"Transactional Outbox for notifications instead of fire-and-forget","gains":"At-least-once delivery guarantee. No silent email loss. Notification delivery is atomic with order state change.","sacrifices":"Adds an outbox table and a polling/CDC relay process. Slightly more operational complexity.","alternatives_considered":["Message broker (RabbitMQ/SQS) — adds infrastructure dependency but stronger delivery semantics","Keep fire-and-forget with scheduled reconciliation job — simpler but reactive rather than proactive"]},{"decision":"Circuit breaker with graceful degradation (accept orders in pending state) vs hard rejection","gains":"Higher order acceptance rate during payment outages. Better customer experience. Orders can be retried when payment recovers.","sacrifices":"Introduces a 'payment-pending' state that needs background processing. Risk of accepting orders that ultimately can't be paid.","alternatives_considered":["Hard reject all orders when circuit is open — simpler but loses revenue during outages","Queue orders for later processing — similar to pending state but with message broker dependency"]}],"flexibility_points":[{"area":"Payment provider","evolution_path":"The ACL in Payment Service means Stripe can be swapped for another gateway (Adyen, Braintree) without changes to Order Service. Add a PaymentGateway interface and swap implementations."},{"area":"Notification channel","evolution_path":"Transactional outbox decouples notification trigger from delivery mechanism. Can add SMS, push notifications, or webhooks by adding consumers to the outbox relay without touching Order Service."},{"area":"Payment flow (sync → async)","evolution_path":"If payment latency or availability becomes untenable, the synchronous call can be migrated to an async saga. The idempotency keys and Payment aggregate state machine already support this — only the coordination pattern changes."}],"rigidity_points":[{"area":"Synchronous Order→Payment→Stripe chain","reason":"By design — we chose synchronous for simplicity and immediate confirmation. This means Order Service uptime is bounded by Payment Service and Stripe uptime. The circuit breaker and graceful degradation mitigate but don't eliminate this coupling."},{"area":"Idempotency key contract","reason":"Once established, the idempotency key format and propagation path must remain stable. Changing it risks double-charges or lost payments. This contract is intentionally rigid."},{"area":"Outbox table schema","reason":"The outbox table is a shared contract between the Order Service write path and the relay process. Schema changes require coordinated deployment."}],"risks":[{"risk":"Double-charging: Retry on timeout where Stripe actually charged but response was lost","severity":"critical","mitigation":"Mandatory idempotency keys on every payment request. Payment Service stores Stripe's idempotency key before calling Stripe. On retry, Stripe deduplicates. Payment Service also deduplicates on its own idempotency key. Order Service must generate a deterministic idempotency key per order (e.g., order-{orderId}-payment-{attempt})."},{"risk":"Silent notification loss: Fire-and-forget HTTP POST drops emails with no trace","severity":"high","mitigation":"Replace fire-and-forget with transactional outbox pattern. (2) Circuit breaker — after 5 failures in 30s, circuit opens for 60s. (3) Bulkhead — dedicated connection pool for payment calls, capped at 50% of total capacity."},{"risk":"Partial failure inconsistency: Payment succeeds at Stripe but Order Service fails to update order status (crash between payment response and DB write)","severity":"high","mitigation":"Payment Service is the source of truth for payment state. Order Service should query Payment Service on startup/recovery to reconcile. A periodic reconciliation job compares Order states with Payment states and fixes inconsistencies."},{"risk":"Thundering herd on circuit close: When circuit breaker transitions from open to half-open, a burst of retried requests can overwhelm a recovering Payment Service","severity":"medium","mitigation":"Half-open state allows only a single probe request.
